metrics.narrative: EY's business problem is clear: they lack a modern, AI-enabled tail-spend and autonomous negotiation offering for their procurement consulting clients, limiting competitiveness in a market where peer consultancies are launching or partnering for these capabilities. Stuart Ogilvie articulated this on the 4 Feb 2026 exploratory call, stating EY needs a white-label platform to serve mid-market and enterprise clients who require supplier negotiation automation and tail-spend visibility without full source-to-pay transformation. However, no quantified impact has been documented: no client pipeline size, no revenue projection for EY from the white-label offering, no cost-of-delay if EY remains without this capability, and no target client ACV or deal count. The Amount field shows 150,000 but it is unclear whether this represents EY's internal investment, a pilot deal value, or a placeholder. The business case exists qualitatively but lacks the financial metrics needed to anchor executive sponsorship or justify partnership resource allocation. Quantifying EY's expected revenue lift, client pipeline size, or competitive loss rate without this capability is the next move.

competition.narrative: Zycus is competing against SAP Ariba (incumbent), two unnamed peer vendors in the 3-vendor shortlist, and an EY proprietary Build option. SAP Ariba is the primary competitor: Next_Step__c states 'they are a big Ariba house', indicating EY's existing SI relationship and client base are deeply embedded in the Ariba ecosystem. This creates high incumbency inertia and a trust bar for Zycus to prove partnership-grade reliability and justify replacing or augmenting Ariba for tail-spend and ANA use cases. The Build option is a real alternative: EY may choose to develop a proprietary solution in-house or via an SI partner, retaining full control over IP, branding, and client commercials. The two unnamed shortlist competitors are unidentified in any call or field, but the 3-vendor evaluation structure means Zycus must differentiate on white-label maturity, multi-tenant architecture, ERP integration flexibility, and proof of prior white-label partnerships. The wedge is Merlin's AI-native autonomous negotiation capability, which is difficult to replicate in a Build scenario and positions Zycus ahead of Ariba (which lacks equivalent AI negotiation agents) if demonstrated effectively. The activation move is to make the Merlin ANA capability the centerpiece of the pending demo, showing EY a live negotiation agent run on a tail-spend supplier scenario that mirrors an EY client use case, and to provide a white-label reference customer or proof point to de-risk the partnership decision and counter the Build option.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics for this white-label partnership have not been defined. No MSA, SOW, or partnership agreement structure has been discussed in any call, field, or task. Given the nature of the deal (white-label SaaS partnership with multi-tenant architecture, revenue share, and ongoing client implementation support), the paper process will be complex: a multi-year partnership agreement covering commercial terms (revenue split, client pricing model, annual minimums or targets), technical responsibilities (hosting, SLA, white-label customization scope, tenant governance), implementation support (who owns client onboarding, training, and ongoing client success), and IP/branding rights. No InfoSec review, ERP integration scoping, or legal redline timeline has been mentioned. The 150,000 Amount may represent an initial pilot or internal EY investment, but the broader partnership commercials are not yet scoped. The contracting path forward is unknown, and should be clarified during the demo or immediately after to avoid a post-selection bottleneck. The recommended move is to surface Zycus's standard white-label partnership agreement template (if one exists) or draft a term sheet covering revenue share, implementation responsibilities, and SLA commitments, then walk EY through it to establish realistic contracting timelines and identify any EY legal or procurement requirements early.

decision_criteria.narrative: EY's evaluation criteria are well-documented from the 4 Feb 2026 exploratory call and subsequent requirement tracking. The must-haves are: (1) white-label offering with EY branding on the Teams front-end, allowing EY to present the platform as their own to clients; (2) configurable single or multi-tenant backend with parent-child client structure, enabling EY to manage multiple client instances with governance controls; (3) integration capability with various client ERP systems (SAP, Oracle, Workday) without requiring clients to adopt Zycus sourcing modules, preserving EY's flexibility to layer Zycus on existing client stacks; (4) governance framework for tenant and update management, supporting client-specific customizations without breaking core platform upgrades; (5) proof that Zycus has delivered white-label solutions before, to de-risk the partnership and demonstrate operational maturity in multi-tenant SaaS partnership models. Stuart Ogilvie emphasized on the 4 Feb call that EY is evaluating 3 vendors plus a Build option, and the decision will hinge on technical feasibility (white-label multi-tenancy), integration complexity, and partnership risk (has the vendor done this before). The Next_Step__c references a QIT pack being prepared for EY's end-of-month decision, indicating these criteria are now being scored. The demo, once scheduled, will validate criteria 1, 2, and 3; criteria 4 and 5 require architecture documents and reference proof points.

value.detail: On 16 June GCA RFP review (Touchpoint 6), Zycus lacks ISO certification: Ellie said 'ISO certification... that's a deal breaker for us because we can't prove we haven't got it.' Anthony replied 'There's a central registry for iSOUR registrations, isn't there?' confirming Zycus is pursuing ISO but does not have it yet. This is a red flag for EY white-label partnership: EY's clients (likely Fortune 500/FTSE enterprises) will require SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR compliance, and pen-test reports. If Zycus cannot provide these, EY cannot onboard clients, and the partnership is blocked. Zycus must clarify: when will ISO audit be complete? Will EY accept in-process certification or require proof before contracting? What other InfoSec/compliance artifacts does EY require?
